debrief.title: High Spinal After Neuraxial Block
debrief.teachingFeedback: A rising spinal blocks sympathetic outflow: SVR and venous return fall, and with T1-T4 spread the cardioaccelerators are blocked, so the usual compensatory tachycardia is replaced by bradycardia. All critical actions were completed without dangerous behavior. Time to treatment: 95s.
teachingPoints: Sympathectomy drops SVR and preload — pressure falls before the airway is threatened.
Cardioaccelerator (T1-T4) block causes bradycardia in the face of hypotension: treat rate and tone together.
Ephedrine or epinephrine treat both rate and pressure; phenylephrine treats tone but can worsen bradycardia.
Dyspnea and hand weakness signal a high (cervical-approaching) level — prepare airway support early.
